coredata.dc:title: Enhanced antibiotic degradation performance of Cd0.5Zn0.5S/Bi2MoO6 S-scheme photocatalyst by carbon dot modification
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shijie
Yan, Ruyu
Cai, Mingjie
Jiang, Wei
Zhang, Mingyi
Li, Xin
coredata.dc:description: S-scheme heterojunction engineering is an effective strategy to attain distinctive photocatalysts. Herein, a carbon dots modulated S-scheme hetero-structured photocatalyst of Cd0.5Zn0.5S nanoparticles/Bi2MoO6 microspheres/carbon dots (CZCBM), aiming to conquer the photo-corrosion and strengthen the photocatalytic property of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, was developed via a facile solvothermal route. Under visible light, the optimal CZCBM-2 affords a 1.8-, 1.5-, or 0.6-time reinforcement in the oxytetracycline degradation rate constant compared to Bi2MoO6, Cd0.5Zn0.5S or Cd0.5Zn0.5S/Bi2MoO6, which is credited to the strengthened visible-light response, increased reactive sites, and efficient dissolution of photo-carriers with optimal redox capacity because of the co-effect of carbon dots and S-scheme heterostructure. Significantly, the photo-corrosion of Cd0.5Zn0.5S is significantly suppressed and CZCBM-2 affords superior stability and reusability during cycling tests. Besides, CZCBM-2 can be well adapted to various environments. The toxicology appraisement unravels the decreased eco-toxicity of most intermediates compared to oxytetracycline. Lastly, an S-scheme charge transfer mechanism with carbon dots as electron reservoir in CZCBM is deduced, which uncloses that •O2 − and h+ dominantly account for oxytetracycline eradication and detoxification. This study demonstrates the design of unique carbon dots favored S-scheme heterostructures as an effective “Two Birds with One Stone” strategy to achieve high anti-photo-corrosion performance and reinforced photocatalytic performance of sulfides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
S-scheme heterojunction
Synergistic effect
Pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs)
Toxicity analysis
Anti-photocorrosion

U1809214 and 51708504 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (Nos. LY20E080014 and LTGN23E080001 ), the Zhejiang Province Industrial Innovation Service Complex "Opening list and Hanging" Project (No. YY-2021C1006), and the Science and Technology Project of Zhoushan (No. 2022C41011).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration for the construction steps of CZCBM catalysts. SEM images of (b) neat BMO, (c) CZS, and (d, e) CZCBM-2; TEM images of (f) CZS and (g, h) CZCBM-2; (i) HRTEM image, (j) EDX spectrum and mapping images of CZCBM-2.
(a) PXRD patterns; (b) FTIR spectra; XPS spectra of (c) Bi 4f and S 2p, (d) Mo 3d, (e) Cd 3d, and (f) Zn 2p.

(a) OTC abatement curves and (b) kinetic plots over various nanomaterials; (c–e) Photocatalytic OTC abatement efficacy of CZCBM-2 under different experimental conditions; (f) Recycling tests of CZS and CZCBM-2 in the destruction of OTC; (g) Photocatalytic OTC abatement efficacy of CZCBM-2 with diverse scavengers; (h, i) ESR signals of reactive radicals.
(a) Decomposition routes of OTC in the CZCBM-2/visible light system; (b–d) Eco-toxicity appraisement.

(a) UV-vis DRS of as-constructed nanomaterials; (b) Plots of (αhν)2 versus the hν (eV), (c) Mott‒Schottky plots, (d) band structure, and (e, f) UPS measurement of BMO and CZS; (g) Transient photocurrent densities, (h) EIS and (i) PL plots of the nanomaterials.
Working mechanism of CZCBM for photocatalytic OTC eradication.

head.ce:title: Enhanced antibiotic degradation performance of Cd0.5Zn0.5S/Bi2MoO6 S-scheme photocatalyst by carbon dot modification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Health Risk Factors for Seafood of Zhejiang Province, National Engineering Research Center for Marine Aquaculture, College of Marine Science and Technology, Zhejiang Ocean University, Zhoushan 316022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Health Risk Factors for Seafood of Zhejiang Province, National Engineering Research Center for Marine Aquaculture, College of Marine Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomass Engineering, Key Laboratory of Energy Plants Resource and Utilization, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biomass Engineering, Key Laboratory of Energy Plants Resource and Utilization, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cInstitute of Biomass Engineering, Key Laboratory of Energy Plants Resource and Utilization, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: S-scheme heterojunction engineering is an effective strategy to attain distinctive photocatalysts. Herein, a carbon dots modulated S-scheme hetero-structured photocatalyst of Cd0.5Zn0.5S nanoparticles/Bi2MoO6 microspheres/carbon dots (CZCBM), aiming to conquer the photo-corrosion and strengthen the photocatalytic property of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, was developed via a facile solvothermal route. Under visible light, the optimal CZCBM-2 affords a 1.8-, 1.5-, or 0.6-time reinforcement in the oxytetracycline degradation rate constant compared to Bi2MoO6, Cd0.5Zn0.5S or Cd0.5Zn0.5S/Bi2MoO6, which is credited to the strengthened visible-light response, increased reactive sites, and efficient dissolution of photo-carriers with optimal redox capacity because of the co-effect of carbon dots and S-scheme heterostructure. Significantly, the photo-corrosion of Cd0.5Zn0.5S is significantly suppressed and CZCBM-2 affords superior stability and reusability during cycling tests. Besides, CZCBM-2 can be well adapted to various environments. The toxicology appraisement unravels the decreased eco-toxicity of most intermediates compared to oxytetracycline. Lastly, an S-scheme charge transfer mechanism with carbon dots as electron reservoir in CZCBM is deduced, which uncloses that •O2 − and h+ dominantly account for oxytetracycline eradication and detoxification. This study demonstrates the design of unique carbon dots favored S-scheme heterostructures as an effective “Two Birds with One Stone” strategy to achieve high anti-photo-corrosion performance and reinforced photocatalytic performance of sulfides.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) has been an increasing threat to eco-environmental health [1–6]. Oxytetracycline (OTC), as one of the most popular broad-spectrum antibiotics, has been extensively utilized in aquaculture and human therapies. However, the increasing occurrence of OTC in waters gives birth to antibiotic resistant microorganisms and other eco-toxicological impacts, threatening the human health. Sunlight-driven photodecomposition of PPCPs into harmless molecules is promising and sustainable for environment purification [7–9]. Despite the eminent potential, the efficacy of current photocatalytic PCPPs eradication remains unsatisfied, owing to the insufficient utilization of sunlight, limited durability and easy reintegration of photo-carriers [10–13]. Thus, devising durable photocatalysts empowering widened photo-response range and efficient charge disassociation is a viable route to improve the pollutant removal efficiency, but it is still a tremendous challenge [14–24].

Very currently, Cd0.5Zn0.5S with the merits of abundant resources, easy synthesis, and intriguing photoelectric properties, has emerged as a prospective chalcogenide photocatalyst. Pitifully, feeble photo-oxidation power, easy photo-carriers reintegration and deteriorated recycling performance are major drawbacks restraining its commercialization [25–28]. The creation of heterojunctions with S-scheme photo-carriers transfer model, which simultaneously enables the efficient photo-carriers detachment and the optimal redox ability, has been demonstrated as a reliable and workable approach to upgrade the efficacy of photocatalysis [29–42]. Recently, our team also devised a fibrous S-scheme photocatalyst of CdS/Ta3N5, which exhibited an efficient photocarriers-separation behavior and strengthened redox capacity in the photocatalytic eradication of pharmaceuticals and Cr(VI) [33].

Bi2MoO6 as an effective visible-light-responsive photocatalyst has been extensively researched in photocatalytic decontamination, disinfection, CO2 reduction and hydrogen production [43–45], credited to its good visible-light response (E g = 2.5−2.8 eV), advantageous layered configuration and distinctive photo-electronic features. Inspiringly, the matched energy level structure enables the combination of Bi2MoO6 with Cd0.5Zn0.5S for building S-scheme heterostructure.

Beyond that, carbon dots (CDs) is a kind of prospective carbon materials by virtue of their ultra-small size, cost-effectiveness, and appealing photoelectric features. CDs has been reckoned as an excellent co-catalyst because it could behave as eletron sink and photosensitizer to boost photoreaction kinetics via enabling effective photo-carrier dissolution and wide-range light absorption [46–53]. Verma et al. fabricated an efficient BiOI/CDs/CdS Z-scheme photocatalytic system for 4-nitrophenol removal, and they found that CDs could largely upgrade the photoactivity and stability [50]. Shafafi et al. developed CDs modified Bi4O5Br2/TiO2 heterojunction for efficient photocatalytic pollutants destruction under visible light [48]. Therefore, simultaneous integration of CDs, Cd0.5Zn0.5S and Bi2MoO6 to create CDs modulated S-scheme system is anticipated to effectively upgrade the photo-activity and photo-stability.

In this research, we incorporated the merits of Bi2MoO6, CDs, and Cd0.5Zn0.5S to build a robust ternary heterojunction photocatalyst, Bi2MoO6/Cd0.5Zn0.5S/carbon dots (CZCBM), for effectively decontaminating antibiotics under visible light. The synergy between CZS/BMO S-scheme configuration and CDs co-catalyst promotes the disintegration and transfer of photo-carriers and photocatalytic antibiotic abatement reaction. More significantly, the CDs assisted S-scheme system can efficaciously protect CZS nanoparticles (NPs) from photo-corrosion, substantially reinforcing the anti-photo-corrosion stability of the photocatalyst.

The detailed preparation method of CZS is shown in Supplementary Information. The construction process of Cd0.5Zn0.5S/carbon dots/Bi2MoO6 (CZCBM) sample is illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). Specifically, Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.25 mmol, 0.1213 g) and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.5 mmol, 0.2425 g) were separately dissolved in ethylene glycol (7 mL). Subsequently, the above two solutions were mixed with stirring for 10 min. After that, CZS (0.1525 g) and a proper amount of CDs were added into 20 mL of ethanol, which underwent ultra-sonication treatment for 60 min. Afterward, the above CZS/CDs suspension and Na2MoO4·2H2O/Bi(NO3)3·5H2O solutions were mixed together with ultra-sonication for 30 min and stirred for 3 h. The resultant suspension was reacted at 160 °C for 24 h in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Finally, the samples with different CDs amounts (1.5, 3, and 9 mg) are named as CZCBM-1, CZCBM-2 and CZCBM-3, respectively.

Generally, 20 mg of catalyst was dispersed in the oxytetracycline (OTC)/levofloxacin (LEV)/tetracycline (TC)/ciprofloxacin (CIP)/norfloxacin (NOR)/enrofloxacin (ENR) solution (60 mL, 20 mg/L). The suspension was completely mixed with stirring in the dark for 30 min. Afterwards, a 300 W Xe lamp (λ > 420 nm) was applied to start the photoreaction in a reaction system at 18 ± 2 °C adjusted by cycling water. The analysis of the PCPPs concentrations was accomplished on a UV-vis spectrophotometer.

The microstructures of the nanomaterials were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 1(b−e)). BMO delivers a hydrangea-like architecture (diameter: ∼1–2.5 μm) consisting of 2D nanosheets with clean surface (Fig. 1(b)). CZS unveils a nanoparticle-shaped structure (size: ∼15–50 nm) (Fig. 1(c) and Fig. S1 in Supplementary Information). After solvothermal treatment, these CZS nanoparticles (NPs) together with carbon dots (CDs) are integrated with BMO microspheres to generate the hierarchical CZCBM heterojunction (Fig. 1(d, e)). To attain in-depth insight into the structures, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were implemented. The TEM image in Fig. 1(f) corroborates the nanoparticle-like architecture (Size: ∼20–40 nm) of CZS. TEM images in Fig. 1(g, h) exhibit that CZCBM-2 has a spherical framework (diameter: ∼1–2.5 μm) and there are plenty of granular CDs/CZS tightly attached on the microsphere, consistent with the SEM observations (Fig. 1(d, e)). The HRTEM image in Fig. 1(i) uncloses that two lattice stripes with spacings of 0.27 and 0.31 nm belong to the orthorhombic phase BMO (200) crystal plane and (101) facet of CZS, respectively. Worth noting, the two kinds of lattices and CDs are compactly connected with each other at the hetero-interface, which is advantageous to the interfacial transport and disintegration of charge carriers. Besides, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum and mapping images of CZCBM-2 illustrate the elemental constituents of Zn, O, Cd, Bi, S, C and Mo atoms and these components are uniformly distributed throughout the microsphere (Fig. 1(j)), further evidencing the successful production of the ternary CZCBM heterostructure.

Powder X-ray powder diffraction (PXRD) was implemented to determine the crystal structure of as-constructed nanomaterials (Fig. 2 (a)). The PXRD of BMO reveals four peaks at 2θ = 28.3°, 32.6°, 46.9°, and 55.4°, assigned to the (131), (002), (260), and (133) facet of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS 76-2388) [45]. The signals at 2θ = 29.3°, 45.6° and 54.2° are assigned to the (101), (110), and (112) facets of Cd0.5Zn0.5S (JCPDS 89-2943). The diffraction peaks of the CZS/BMO heterojunction match well with both the orthorhombic BMO and hexagonal CZS phases. All the CZCBM heterojunctions deliver the PXRD pattern similar to that of CZS/BMO, which is ascribed to the low content and highly dispersive state of CDs.

FT-IR measurement was taken to unclose the chemical composition of CZCBM as well as confirm the presence of CDs in the heterostructure (Fig. 2(b)). The FT-IR spectrum of BMO unveils the distinct peaks located at 560, 730 and 841 cm−1, belonging to Bi–O, Mo–O and Mo–O–Mo bonds, respectively. With respect to CDs, the absorption bands at about 1195, 1550, 1396 and 1670 cm−1 are assigned to the C–O, C=C, C–H and C=O groups [34]. Evidently, the spectrum of CZCBM-2 exhibits the peaks of both BMO and CDs. No CZS peaks detected are accredited to its low absorption band intensity. The result certifies the existence of CDs in CZCBM-2.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was implemented to scrutinize the chemical states and interfacial interactions of the CZCBM-2 heterojunction. The whole XPS spectrum manifests that CZCBM-2 is made up of Cd, Bi, Zn, Mo, C and O, reflecting the co-existence of BMO, CDs and CZS (Fig. S2(a)). This finding is consistent with the results of EDS and EDX mapping (Fig. 1(j)). The Bi 4f spectrum of CZCBM-2 reveals two peaks at 158.95 and 164.25 eV, assigned to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 orbitals (Fig. 2(c)) [54,55]. The S 2p spectrum of CZCBM-2 reveals two typical peaks of S2–, namely S 2p3/2 (161.25) and S 2p1/2 (162.35 eV) (Fig. 2(c)) [56–59]. The Mo 3d spectrum can be de-convoluted into two peaks belonging to Mo 3d5/2 (232.3 eV) and Mo 3d3/2 (235.4 eV) of Mo6+ (Fig. 2(d)) [60]. In the Cd 3d spectrum, the peaks located at 405.15 and 411.85 eV are accredited to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 of Cd2+, respectively (Fig. 2(e)) [34,61,62]. The Zn 2p spectrum exhibits the typical signal of Zn2+ in CZCBM-2, including Zn 2p3/2 (1021.75 eV) and Zn 2p1/2 (1044.85 eV) (Fig. 2(f)) [34,63]. Four distinct peaks of C=C (284.6 eV), C–O (285.35 eV), C=O (286.55 eV) and O=C–O bonds (288.35 eV) are also identified in the C 1s spectrum, confirming the existence of carbon dots (Fig. S2(b)) [48]. The O 1s is composed of three signals at 529.6, 531.15 and 532.75 eV, belonging to lattice oxygen and –OH groups, and H2O, respectively (Fig. S2(c)) [64]. It is well-known that an enhancement (weakness) of the electron density could give birth to a drop (rise) in binding energy. As is obvious, the positive shift of peaks (Cd, Zn and S) in CZCBM-2 than CZS reflects that CZS acts as an electron donor. Whereas the negative movement of peaks (Bi and Mo) in CZCBM-2 than BMO implies BMO serves as an electron harvester. The above XPS finding offers significant evidence for the triumphant construction of CZCBM-2 heterojunction with intensely interacting interface.

The OTC photo-destruction tests were done to judge the photocatalytic behavior of CZCBM catalysts under visible light. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), the photocatalytic OTC eradication efficiency of various catalysts complies with the sequence: CZCBM-2 (84.8%) > CZCBM-1 (78%) > CZCBM-3 (69.4%) > CZS/BMO (64.2%) > CZS (54.7%) > Mixture (52.6%) > BMO (45.1%). Worth noting, the ternary CZCBM heterojunctions outperform BMO, CZS and CZS/BMO, signifying that the photocatalytic property of CZS is substantially upgraded after integrating with CDs and BMO simultaneously. The catalytic behavior of CZCBM heterojunctions is dependent on the content of CDs because of the varied roles: optimized CDs content enables the accelerated detachment of photo-carriers at the hetero-interface, while redundant CDs may give rise to a decrement of available reactive centers and visible-light utilization of CZCBM. The highest efficiency of OTC elimination is achieved by CZCBM-2, which also greatly outperforms the mechanically mixed sample, certifying the eminent role of intensely connected hetero-interface in boosting the photocatalytic behavior [33]. The pseudo-first-order model was employed to fit the photo-destruction data (Fig. 3(b)). Evidently, the optimal CZCBM-2 achieves 0.0387 min‒1 for OTC eradication, with 1.5-, 1.8- and 0.6-time reinforcement compared to CZS (0.0156 min‒1), BMO (0.0136 min‒1) and CZS/BMO (0.0237 min‒1), respectively.

To assess its actual applicability, some cardinal factors that could impact the catalytic property of CZCBM-2 were also considered (Fig. 3(c–e)). The photocatalytic behavior of CZCBM-2 at various pH values was studied (Fig. 3(c)). The eradication percentage of OTC is approximately 87.6%, 84.8%, 92.4% and 85.1% at pH=5, pH=6.6, pH=9 and pH=11, signifying the good pH adaptability of CZCBM-2. The OTC decontamination tests with co-existing humic acid (HA) at 0–15 mg/L were carried out (Fig. 3(d)) and the result indicates HA exhibits an obstructive impact on OTC decontamination over CZCBM-2, which is originated from its photon-shielding effect and capture of reactive oxidative species (ROS) (Fig. 3(d)) [34]. It is noted that the presence of NaCl, NaHCO3, NaNO3 and Na2SO4 salts in the system hardly affects the OTC destruction over CZCBM-2 (Fig. 3(e)). Nevertheless, the OTC abatement with Na3PO4 co-existing is substantially inhibited than other salts, which is attributed to that PO4 3– preferably captures photo-produced ROS [34,35].

The high-efficiency mineralization of organic contaminants to reduce the risk of secondary pollution is significant in industrial applications. Noticeably, the TOC concentrations of OTC solution significantly decrease as the reaction proceeds. The mineralization ratio of OTC within 120 min of reaction over CZCBM-2 is determined as 60.9% (Fig. S3), reflecting that OTC can be efficiently eliminated and mineralized by CZCBM-2. This finding reveals that CZCBM-2 holds impressive mineralization behavior.

The cycle test on OTC destruction was taken to appraise the reusability of CZS and CZCBM-2 (Fig. 3(f)). Obviously, the catalytic efficiency of CZS drops remarkably after five runs due to the unfavorable photo-corrosion. Encouragingly, the CZCBM-2 is proved to be efficient for five cycles of OTC decontamination without noticeable decay, certifying CZCBM-2 holds long-term reusability and durability. Besides, the PXRD patterns of the CZS and CZCBM-2 after cycling tests were recorded to appraise the structural stability (Fig. S4). Clearly, the crystalline structure of the recycled CZS experiences a distinct change, which results from the generation of metal Cd (JCPDS No. 05-0674) and ZnS (JCPDS No. 05-0566) phases. Such structural deterioration is originated from the photo-corrosion of CZS during cycling tests (Fig. S4(a)). By contrast, the recycled CZCBM-2 reveals that no perceptible change in the PXRD pattern is detected (Fig. S4(b)), confirming that the crystal structure of CZCBM-2 is highly robust. The substantially strengthened stability of CZCBM-2 originates from the carbon dots adjusted S-scheme configuration, which enables the efficient depletion of photo-generated holes in CZS and thus effectively suppresses the photo-corrosion.

The CZCBM-2/vis system is also effective in the destruction of other antibiotics (e.g., tetracycline, levofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin and enrofloxacin) (Fig. S5), reflecting its universal adaptability for various antibiotics eradication.

Scavenging experiments were done to unveil the roles of ROS in OTC eradication over CZCBM-2 (Fig. 3(g)). As is obvious, the quenching of •OH by isopropanol (IPA) has an auxiliary impact on OTC destruction efficiency (about 13.1% reduction). By contrast, the introduction of (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyl-1-oxide) TEMPO and ammonium oxalate (AO) for capturing •O2 – and h+ makes the OTC elimination suppressed by 31.3% and 43.8%, respectively, signifying that •O2 – and h+ make significant contributions to OTC decontamination. The involvement and creation of •O2 − and •OH radicals for photocatalytic antibiotic destruction were further evidenced by the electron spin resonance (ESR) tests (Fig. 3(h, i)) [22,29,33]. Noticeably, CZCBM-2 delivers the prominent signals of DMPO−•O2 − and DMPO−•OH, evidencing the eminent photo-redox capacity of the hetero-structured system for the creation of multiple •O2 − and •OH. This finding provides evidence for the conjecture that the photo-created charge carriers dissociate and redistribute in an S-scheme route, coincident with the XPS detections (Fig. 2(c-f)) [22,29,33].

LC-MS technique was adopted to analyze the degradation products and deduce the transformation routes of OTC in the CZCBM-2 photoreaction process (Fig. S6). On account of the photocatalysis mechanism and intermediates (Fig. S5 and Table S1 in Supplementary Information), we propose two transformation pathways of OTC (Fig. 4 (a)). In pathway I, the loss of –CO on the ring structure contributes to the generation of product P1 (m/z = 433). Under the assault of •OH species, the dihydroxylation, deamidization and the detachment of dimethylamino group give birth to P2 (m/z = 349). Afterwards, the dihydroxylation, demethylation, together with ring cleavage take place for generating P3 (m/z = 166) and P4 (m/z = 129). In pathway II, the demethylation reaction takes place for generating P5 (m/z = 433), which is transformed to P6 (m/z = 336) through the deamidation, deamination and dehydration processes. Subsequently, the P6 is decomposed into P7 (m/z = 203) and P8 (m/z = 141) via C–C bond cleavage. Furthermore, P5 undergoes the ring-opening and dehydroxylation processes to produce P9 (m/z = 277). Further demethylation, dehydroxlation and C–C bond breakage happen for producing P10 (m/z = 248), P11 (m/z = 153) and P12 (m/z = 100). Ultimately, these intermediates could be broken into small molecules through fierce photoreactions.

The toxicity estimation based on T.E.S.T. was performed via exploring the acute toxicity, developmental toxicity and mutagenicity of OTC and its intermediates (Fig. 4(b–d) and Table S2) [65]. As exhibited in Fig. 4(b), the daphnia magna LC50 value of OTC is 4.29 mg/L, which is deemed as “toxic”. Almost all the intermediates exhibit a higher LC50 value than OTC except P1, P6 and P10, signifying a considerable decline in the toxicity. Besides, the developmental toxicity of OTC is 0.88, which is greater than most of the products except P7 and P10 (Fig. 4(c)). Furthermore, the mutagenicity of all the products is considerably attenuated compared with OTC (Fig. 4(d)). Impressively, P3–6, P8 and P12 belong to “mutagenic negative”. The above findings reflect that the CZCBM-2/visible light system holds the potential for the effective mitigation of bio-toxicity of OTC. The findings of the above photocatalytic tests evidence the eminent potential of the CZCBM heterostructure for antibiotic wastewater remediation.

UV-Vis DRS was recorded to evaluate the light-responsive properties of CZCBM heterojunctions (Fig. 5 (a)). BMO and CZS exhibit typical absorption edge of approximately 462 [45] and 535 nm [28,34], respectively. Benefitting from the CDs, the ternary heterojunctions all manifest a broader range of light absorption, demonstrating the robust light harvesting ability [53]. The energy band-gap (E g) of CZS and BMO attained from the Kubelka-Munk equation is ∼2.28 and ∼2.76 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(b)). Combined with conduction-band positions (E CB) of CZS (–0.81 V) and BMO (–0.35 V) from the Mott‒Schottky (M–S) curves (Fig. 5(c)), their corresponding valence-band positions (E VB) are 1.47 and 2.41 V vs. NHE by E VB – E CB = E g. Correspondingly, the band alignment diagram of CZCBM is presented in Fig. 5(d). UPS measurement was implemented to obtain the work function (ϕ). Generally, a work function (ϕ) could be calculated by the equation, ϕ = hv – (E cutoff – E fermi). The He I energy is 21.22 eV, hence the ϕ values of BMO and CZS are determined as 5.83 and 5.09 eV through the UPS measurement (Fig. 5(e, f)). Noticeably, the work function of BMO is larger than that of CZS, reflecting that the aggregated electrons in CZS flood into BMO upon the hybridization of them. The above characterizations indicate the migration mode of charge carriers in the CZCBM junction should obey S-scheme heterojunction.

The transport and dissolution dynamics of photo-carriers were studied to unclose the reasons for the superior photocatalytic OTC removal performance of CZCBM-2. The transient photocurrent response analysis demonstrates that CZCBM-2 delivers a pronouncedly strengthened photocurrent intensity compared with BMO, CZS and CZS/BMO (Fig. 5(g)), implying the more effective detachment and migration of the photo-carriers after the incorporation of carbon dots into the heterojunction [33,66–70]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed to unclose the charge-transport behavior of the CZCBM-2. As depicted in Fig. 5(h), the semicircle of CZCBM-2 is the smallest among the tested samples, verifying the substantial reinforcement of the interfacial charge-transfer capability is achieved when the CDs are embedded into CZS/BMO [34,71–73]. Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) test was implemented (Fig. 5(i)) and the result shows that the PL intensity of CZCBM-2 achieves the minimum, confirming that CZCBM-2 has the lowest e––h+ reunion efficiency [35,74–77]. The results of TPR, EIS and PL corroborate that the integration of BMO with CDs/CZS to construct CDs-assisted S-scheme photocatalyst enables an eminent upgrade of the charge dissociation/migration property, guaranteeing an increased amount of photo-carriers to participate in antibiotic destruction (Fig. 6 (g–i)).

In the light of above analyses, the working mechanism of CZCBM for PCPPs photo-degradation is explicit (Fig. 6). At the interface, the difference in work functions gives birth to a directed interfacial electric field (IEF) accompanied by the band-edge bending of CZS and BMO. Clearly, the interfacial band-edge bending restrains the easy transportation of photocreated electrons from the CB of CZS to that of BMO. Besides, the IEF in the direction from CZS to BMO supplies a robust driving force to spatially disintegrate and accumulate the powerful electrons in CZS and holes in BMO, respectively [29,33,35,78]. Worth noting, CDs not only serves as a superb charge migration pathway to promote the S-scheme photocarriers dissolution (Fig. 6), but also acts as a fovarable photosensitizer to upgrade the light response capability of the heterostructure (Fig. 5(a)) [49,53,78]. Consequently, the CDs favored S-scheme configuration of CZCBM enables the improved sunlight absorption and detachment of photo-carriers with maximum redox power for creation of multiple ROS, thus achieving the extraordinary photocatalytic performance toward the destruction of antibiotics.

A novel ternary S-scheme heterostructure of CZCBM was fabricated via a feasible solvothermal reaction, enabling 1.6, 2.5 and 2.8 folds antibiotic degradation rate constant of that for CZS/BMO, CZS and BMO. Significantly, the photo-corrosion of CZS is conquered effectively, endowing CZCBM with strengthened robustness and stability. The eminent photocatalytic performance benefits from the creation of carbon dots regulated S-scheme heterojunction, which favors spatial migration and disintegration of photo-carriers and reinforces sunlight absorption. Noteworthily, CZCBM also manifests a universal and high photo-activity towards the elimination of other emerging pharmaceutical antibiotics. This research could give some significant insights into the development of efficient and stable sulfide-based photocatalysts for eradicating PPCPs through the combination of S-scheme heterojunction with carbon dots.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1809214 and 51708504), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (Nos. LY20E080014 and LTGN23E080001), the Zhejiang Province Industrial Innovation Service Complex "Opening list and Hanging" Project (No. YY-2021C1006), and the Science and Technology Project of Zhoushan (No. 2022C41011).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2023.05.009.
